description: Thursday 4 December 2025 | Economic Development Science and Innovation Committee 2024/2025 Annual review of Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment Hearing of evidence: Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment Suzanne Stew, Acting Chief Executive Nic Blakeley, Deputy Secretary, Labour, Science and Enterprise Richard Griffiths, Deputy Secretary, Corporate and Digital Shared Services Alison McDonald, Deputy Secretary, Immigration Robert Pigou, Deputy Secretary, Regional Development & Commercial Services Melanie Porter, Deputy Secretary, Strategy and Assurance Paul Stocks, Deputy Secretary, Building, Resources and Markets Ingrid Bayliss, Acting Deputy Secretary, Te Whakatairanga Service Delivery Lesley McConnell, Chief Advisor to the Secretary Paul Helm, Chief Financial Officer Jeannie Melville, Deputy Chief Operating Officer Immigration Simon Thomas, Head of Building System Delivery & Assurance Andy Hume, General manager, Commerce, Consumer and Business Jivan Grewal, General Manager, Workplace Relations and Safety Policy Danette Olsen, General Manager, Science System Investment and Performance Fiona Whiteridge, General Manager, Refugee and Migrant Services Steve Watson, General Manager, Immigration Compliance and Investigations

Thank you. Good morning.

See yourself on the screen. (laughs) So when you're ready we'll get started, so we'll just give 30 seconds. Yep. Just make sure. Can I grab you a coffee now and then? Would be wonderful. Okay, sure. That would be good. All right. Thank you, so much. Um, so, um, we will start with opening remarks.

Uh, opening remarks, if you could keep them to around five minutes, that would be really good. And i- any of your team members, I know that many of you will be coming forward to the front table to answer questions. Um, so that is a best practice. So if you have a question that you feel like we want to answer, please come to the table and speak into the microphone. And that is important for our staff. Thank you.

Thank you. Uh, Tēnā koutou and, and thank you for inviting us this morning. I'm the acting Secretary and Chief Executive, Suzanne Sturge. And today, I'm joined by the rest of the MBIE senior leadership team, and the general managers from the areas that you have indicated you'd like us to focus on. With me at the table currently is the Deputy Secretary for Corporate and Digital Shared Services, Richard Griffiths- Morning.

And on my left, the Chief Financial Officer, Paul Helm. The other members will introduce themselves as they come up to the table. Firstly, this morning, I would like to acknowledge our former chief executive, Carolyn Tremain. Carolyn left MBIE in late September after eight years as our chief executive. Carolyn made an enormous contribution to MBIE and the wider public service.

And her leadership has been instrumental in guiding MBIE through complex challenges and delivering outcomes in support of strengthening New Zealand's economy and our communities. Her commitment to public service values and her ability to foster collaboration across government made her an outstanding leader. I recognize that at this review this morning, we have big shoes to fill, um, but look forward with the outstanding help of the team to answering your questions this morning.

Now, MBIE's focus remains on supporting the delivery of government priorities and delivering outstanding services to the public. As an organization, we are right-sized with high-performing people delivering value-for-money services. Our main responsibilities span 19 interconnected ministerial portfolios with support for several more across three main funding areas. We also have responsibility for 17 regulatory systems with considerable variation in scope, function, and risk. These systems underpin economic activity and

growth and provide safety and certainty to people, businesses, and the country as a whole. In early 2025, the government launched Going for Growth, and we have been working at pace in support of this priority across its five pillars, developing talent, competitive business settings, promoting global trade and investment, innovation, technology and science, and infrastructure for growth. This includes delivering a broad suite of actions from immigration and workforce initiatives to infrastructure acceleration, investment attraction, and even science

reforms. As an organization, MBIE's efficiency initiatives focused on structural simplification, reducing organizational layers, and consolidating functions to streamline our decision-making. Strong fiscal discipline drove cost savings in such areas as contractors and consultants, property rationalization, as well as overall operational efficiencies.

Savings include a departmental baseline reduction of $56 million and non-departmental savings of $152 million achieved through ending and scaling back programs that were no longer aligned with the government's priorities. A major emphasis of our work in MBIE is ensuring that we are well-placed for the future with our investment in digital transformation. This includes automation and AI tools to improve our productivity and reduce administrative overheads. These changes were underpinned by our refreshed strategic framework, ensuring alignment with government priorities while building

capability and resilience in our workforce. Since we last met the committee, we have also delivered 158 Cabinet papers, contributed to the introduction of 12 government bills to Parliament, and delivered over 4,000 briefings. I also want to acknowledge some of the key items from the government priorities that our people have successfully achieved this year. These include actions as part of Going for Growth.

In support of developing talent, our actions include adjusting the immigration settings to attract skilled workers and regional investment to grow regional economies by improving resilience and increasing productivity.In order to improve our competitive business settings, we have progressed reforms to the Holidays Act and Employment Relations Act, progressed legislation to streamline financial services regulation, and conducted reviews of the electricity, banking, and the grocery sectors.

The focus on promotion of global trade investment saw us supporting the establishment of investment... Invest New Zealand as a one-stop shop for foreign direct investment, and developing the tourism growth roadmap to double tourism export value by 2034. The innovation, technology, and science pillar, all about connecting science and technology systems to business needs and enabling innovation across our economy.

And we have supported reform of the science system to create four new public research organizations and established the Prime Minister's Science Innovation and Technology Advisory Council. In support of infrastructure for growth, our actions included facilitating a resilient energy system, approving investments through the Regional and Infrastructure Fund, and supporting the streamlining of building consents and the use of overseas building products to lower costs and speed up delivery. We also supported the delivery of major

regulatory changes, which included initiating consultation on the Holidays Act reform and the clarification of contractor employment status, as well as progressing regulatory changes to remove the GE ban and enable safe use of gene technology. In our energy and resources area, we supported the Offshore Renewable Energy Bill, the Crown Minerals Amendment Bill, and the Minerals Strategy under review of the electricity market performance and provided advice on reforms.

Finally, (laughs) I'd like to highlight some of the achievements of our service delivery functions. We continue to focus on delivering high-quality, value-for-money services for our customers. Our customer satisfaction exceeded 75% across more than 20 services, including tenancy, dispute resolution, and immigration.

In the last year, we handled over 1.3 million calls in our service centers with wait times under our five-minute target, a result I know that many of my private sector colleagues would be envious of. We've achieved our visa processing efficiency targets across student visa, work visa, and employer accreditation applications, and 97% of Migrant Exploitation Protection visas are assessed and completed within five days. Even earlier this week, the Tenancy Bond Services Team introduced a new IT platform, replacing our old 25-year-old Bond 2000 paper-based

system. Not only does it support pet bonds, but enables faster service for landlords, for tenants, and for our staff. I'm really excited about the achievement of this major platform, so hopefully you'll give us some opportunity later to share more on that. We've also strengthened our compliance activities across all our regulatory systems, and the teams are working really hard to ensure that we have a fair playing field for everyone in New Zealand.

The quality of work that MBIE has done this year and continues to do is testament to our fantastic people. I know that our people have worked incredibly hard and at peace once again this year. So I and the senior leadership team would like to thank them for all that they have done this year in support of our purpose to grow New Zealand for all.

We are very proud of what they have achieved. Thank you very much for your indulgence, and we're happy to answer all your questions. Thank you so much for those opening remarks. And, um, there is a structured agenda which has been, I believe, shared with you. So we will follow that. So, so members, the first one is whole of organization focus. Uh, Arena Williams.

(inaudible) Hey, thank you all for coming in and acknowledge, um, many of you who have made your time available to the committee today for our scrutiny session. It is a long one, but an important one for us. And acknowledge your comments, uh, about Caroline. Thank you for, um, bringing more attention to what is a stellar career in, in public service. Um, many people who will be looking at this review will understand the workings of the organization, but looking at it as a whole for ordinary New Zealanders, um, you might be

forgiven for, um, being confused about the per- performance measures, particularly around the performance targets changing this year. Why are the targets, um, seemingly becoming easier to hit, um, and what are you doing to make sure that your focus on delivery, which you began with, is something that New Zealanders can have faith in?

So I wouldn't say that we, we go about looking at targets to make them easier to hit. I think what we look at is, are the targets that we have truly reflective of the work that we're doing and are they, are they actually measuring the best outcome? So yes, we will have changed a number of our measures and that's because we want to ensure that the measures we have accurately reflect the impact and truly reflect the impact that we're having. Um, whether or not you think they're easier to hit, I would also say we're probably getting better at hitting them.

(laughs) So, um, as we improve our performance, it might look that they're getting easier to hit, but they're actually stretch targets we're working really, really hard on achieving. Um, it's one of those things that the team, I think, have a relentless focus on, how can we lift our performance? And part of that was, was done by doing some of the changes around our performance strategy for this year, really looking hard at ourselves about, how do we measure?

Also, how do we get access to data? Uh, maybe, um, Paul or Richard might want to follow up. But what we have done is, how do we get access to real-time data or near real-time data to enable us to improve our performance? And that's where you'll see us use the likes of digital tools and, and the improvements we've made in our data space so that we can actually respond much quicker.

Mm-hmm. So yes, it may look that the targets maybe are getting easier, but actually what is actually happening is we're getting h- working harder to achieve them, I think, and doing a better job on that. Just wanna dig into that, and I'm not trying to catch you in any of these targets. Mm-hmm. I wanna appreciate what's going on. Some, you might appreciate, would be in response to a change in government priorities. Can you give us ex- an example of those and how they've changed?

Some, um, might be because they were un- they weren't hit last year and they were un- unachievable. Can you give us an example of those? Okay. Give me a minute and... (laughs) Or, a- and- and as well, I'm interested here in the broader picture of, um, if we want MBIE to be delivering on, um, you know, from, from a basis of having targets that we understand and performance measures that demonstrate not only to New Zealanders, but to the rest of the public service- Mm-hmm.

... that MBIE is delivering value and are victory to the, uh, delivery focus. Hmm. Um, how, how do we get a sense of that from a system-wide view? I think some of that is changing, um, if you think about changing behaviors. So, one, I know some of the things we are looking at in our service delivery space, for example, going forward, is we'll have, we have targets which say we will continually grow the number of people who will visit, for example, a website. Well, the way things are going now with the use of AI- Right.

... people are less likely to come directly to your website. What you actually want to be is the answer to the question that when someone asks AI what the question is, you're the answer to it, and you're the, you're the actual authoritative source for that answer. And so, as, so that's one of the... I, I can give you an example of why you would say, "Oh, well, that would be an easy thing to change.

You'll, you'll just lower the bar and see you don't need to have so many people coming." Well, actually, they're not going to come directly. What we need to u- do is use better tools to ensure that we are the, we are the authoritative source for those questions, and that people aren't being given the wrong answer. Which is, so you'll see us adjust where we start to see things like that change. So it's not that we're trying to say we don't want to do a great work, we don't want to ensure that we're raising awareness for the

public. It's about us recognizing that technology has changed, people's behaviors have changed, and therefore we have to measure it a different way. That I would need to go and start looking through for some others for you, but that's, that's the kind of thing where you will see us change our approach to how we measure. So, so am I getting the right idea that the targets aren't a focus for you?

They are a focus. Oh, yeah. Oh my goodness, yes. And that's why I talked about, right at the start, about our ongoing investment in our data and technology to make sure that we have the best information at our fingertips to ensure we are monitoring our progress and making sure we're doing the right thing. Can, can I add a comment? Um, so, so we have a program in place with regards to improving the performance measures and actually, uh, hitting those targets. Um, so that involves logic mapping through.

It goes through which ones we think we can actually achieve relatively sooner than later. Right. Uh, the logic mapping actually helps transfer from inputs, outputs, to outcomes, because what we found was in hi- historically, a lot of the measure had been output-focused rather than outcome-focused and impact. So, in working through our program, um, and we've made improvements on, I think that was acknowledged by Audit New Zealand as well, um, we are systematically just working through the ones we believe that need work, uh,

changing the thinking from logic mapping from, "If this is having an impact, what does that look like? What would be the appropriate measures in place?" And then transferring into those. Uh, there's still a number of years of work, I think, still, to actually get through all of our performance measures. And as the government changes outcomes or changes funding, uh, sometimes we see them drop away, sometimes funding drops away.

But yeah, that's an absolute, uh, focus for us, for, uh, particularly with the finance and performance team. Right. A new family from, uh, Honorable Jenny Anderson. Thank you very much, uh, Madam Chair. Um, I, I was just interested to ask a couple questions around, um- Go ahead.... product, labor productivity, if that's okay? Mm-hmm. Um, I note that there was sort of no, um, sort of positive shifts across the four outcomes, as we've noted.

Um, and it, and that there was sort of negative trends in real household median weekly income, um, labor productivity growth, and also, uh, employment in new businesses. So, so we have a structured agenda here. Oh, right. So I thought it was like overview. Yeah. Is this- So we are on Part One. So if, if you can bring it to the whole of organization, because we have labor productivity in Part Two.

Well, as an all of organization, what did you do to improve real household median weekly income, um, in terms of, uh, what, what kinds of steps went towards- Oh, We'll go straight to policy. Okay. So there's only, um, so much that, uh, departments can do in the short term.

The real, um, productivity is really a long-term, uh, challenge. Um, and it's been an ongoing challenge for New Zealand for, for decades actually. Our productivity has remained low, and the gap between us and, and, you know, other- Mm-hmm.... um, advanced economies has been growing. So it's a, it's, uh, very much a long-term challenge.

And so the solution to that is to look at whatever the medium-term policy settings that could change to, uh, to it, to, to change that trajectory. And so the, the key thing that we do as a, as MBIE, is our, um, policy work to address those, uh- (clears throat) ... medium-term settings that are likely to put that on a different trajectory.

And that goes to the, to the Going For Growth agenda that- Mm-hmm.... um, Suzanne's talked about and, uh, MBIE's role in some of those programs. Just from your, from, from, from your own understanding, what, what do you think really, if you could summarize, are the key factors that, that New Zealand does need to address, not, not just in the medium term, but overall what's holding us back the most in terms of that productivity?

I think there's a whole- Can I just interrupt there? So just want to be very, very clear that you are asking question about the productivity of MBIE because this is whole of organization. So labor productivity is in Part Two. So we haven't moved to Part Two yet. Okay. Well, I, I'm not there yet, and it's kind of on, I'm not a- able to ask questions then. And I, it does relate to the whole organiz- We do have a structured agenda, I mean, one of the core functions of MBIE, I would assume, would be- So if you focus it on MBIE's productivity, that's why we're

here. Okay. So, uh, as you're, as an all-of organization goal to increase the labor productivity- (laughs)... of New Zealand, what do you foresee as being one of the inhibiting factors of us reaching, um, a, comparative with other countries- Do you want us to, but can we talk about MBIE's productivity? Sure. So for the, um, uh, for the economy, um, the...

If I go right back to the... Probably- Sorry, no. I- I- I'm- I'm- I am interested in my productivity. So, but, um- That is part two. So, we haven't moved up to part two. Well, So, I don't want to repeat this again. And what- what time do you get to part two, do you think? So, I think y- y- you need to have access to papers if you are on the committee. And I... Any other members have questions here? A new primary from Dr.

Vanessa Bennett. I make the question, please. Hold on, hold on. A new primary from Dr. Vanessa Bennett. Thank you, madam chair. Um, thank you very much for your overview. Um, it's- it's quite, um, a huge organization, obviously, um, and, uh, a- and that comes with its own, um, benefits and some challenges.

Um, I think if we are looking at the, um, the overall organization, one of the things that always, a question that always comes to me is how... What are the mechanisms that you keep, um, the whole team together with? How, uh... How do you, um... How do you manage to keep all of the different departments, um, in one place without having little empires being built all over the show?

Okay. Um, I- I- I appreciate that from the outside, sometimes MB can look a little bit, you know, h- a- quite big. Mm-hmm. Um, first of all, start with the common purpose. And so, if there's one thing that I can say about, um, our MB people, and they are all behind a common purpose, and if you put that at the forefront, it's always easier to rally everyone together with a common thing that you're aiming at. At the end, I think around me, we are surrounded by a really, really strong leadership team who, uh, work together-

Mm-hmm.... um, settle problems and challenges together. And that enables us with a common plan, with a common strategy to actually work together around, how do we bring the pieces to focus on things? The- the benefit of MB is, is that, right? It can look across the system, multiple systems- Mm-hmm.

... and see where are the gaps, where there overlaps, and how can we ensure that we manage those- those gaps or overlaps as best as we can and as efficiently as we can. And so, I think what we do is... I'll give you an example. So, for our compliance teams, and it may look that we have got rather, um, different compliance teams in terms of we have immigration compliance teams, we have labor inspectorate, we have tenancy compliance teams, we have compliance teams around the companies.

But if you think about it, they are all looking at ensuring there's a fair playing field for people in New Zealand. Um, when they work together on a common... And you'll see us now where we have common, um, operations, we bring to bear the- the actual understanding of our... In the round. Yeah.

How can we help businesses? Both how can we help businesses and employees and employers, but also, how can we hold bad actors to account? I give you an example of something we did, uh, about a year ago where instead of each of those business units going and talking to businesses before the ski season, they went together.

They went to see, um, businesses in Queenstown and said, "From an employment point of view, this is what you need to think about when you're bringing in migrant workers for the ski season. From the migration, immigration point of view, these are the visa considerations you need to think about. From the tenancy or housing point of view, here's the things you need to think about." So, instead of expecting businesses to go to three different sessions, MB came as one and said, "Here's how we can help you across the board." And that was

exceptionally well-received. So, I think- Yeah. ... how we bring it together is by that unrelenting focus on why we're here, what's the purpose for the organization, and a culture that says we work together as one MB. That's... And to be honest, we have fantastic people who are relentlessly focused on that every day. And so, that actually makes it easier for us.

Can I, can I add a couple- Mm-hmm.... of extra comments? So, the- the way the organization is structured with respect to its governance and oversight. Uh, so we have a number of committees. So, we have a finance investment committee that's chaired by deputy secretaries, but we also, uh, use our general managers and their cross-functional team. Mm-hmm. So, rather than just have a vertical silo, so the- the, uh, chair of our finance investment committee isn't me and isn't Paul.

(laughs) It's one of our other DCEs. Mm-hmm. Um, a- and that's a little bit around ensuring that we are working horizontally, to your point- Yeah. ... as opposed to, uh, working in the silos. Um, so we have finance investment. We have a- a risk and audit, uh, committee. We've got a- a people committee. We've got, um, a team that looks after all of our projects. But again, they're all cross-functional teams.

Um, we've got a really comprehensive people strategy as well, so looks at, uh, leadership capability- Mm-hmm.... and our people lead capability. They are done in forums with our, again, cross-functional, so that we've got that network kind of approach. We actually run, uh, um, sessions called Network, which is for our people leaders to actually, one, get to know each other, but share some of the common issues they may be facing. And of course, we've also got our engagement survey, uh, which, uh, is- is, uh, well, uh, responded to,

and highlights some of the actions. We take those as well with regards to... And they can be things focusing on leadership, communications. There's weekly, uh, blogs that go out and vlogs that go out from the chief executive. And we run regular, uh, huis with our people, but also with, um, our, uh, next layer down leadership teams too at three- threes and fours. Actually, if I can just chip in. Uh, you mentioned our engagement survey.

Um, we take really seriously what our people feed back to us. Mm-hmm. And one of the areas, as you mentioned, uh, two of the questions they gave us was, "The people in my team collaborate to get the work done, and the people in my team are encouraged to come up with new and better ways of doing things." Where we are... I mean, we're sitting up in the 87%, which is above the rest of the public service.

And I- I'll tell you, from my days in the private sector, I'd have loved those (laughs) results too. (laughs) So, we have a team that na- it's a culture that is of natural collaboration. Yeah. Mm-hmm. And, um, just to supplement on that, um, what's, uh, what's the state of your IT systems in terms of helping to support the culture and the work in, um, in the organization?

So, we invest quite a bit. I'm gonna stop Richard chomping at the bit- (laughs)... to tell you all about AI. Um, we invest significantly in our IT systems. Uh-I'll save the, the, the tenancy one for later. But, uh, not only for systems to enable our customers, but also to enable our people. And I've given Richard enough pause now to then go on to talk about- (laughs)... where it's (inaudible) our investments in IT.

Sure. So- Thank you. Um, i- it's a great question. So, MBIE is a combination of a number of, uh, agencies. And yes, it's 12 years old, but, um, so there's a collection of different databases, collection of different systems. Some are very old, some are new. And, uh, so our approach has always been to modernize, manage what we call the tech debt side of things, but also look to upgrade systems, make sure they're safe and secure.

We have a three to five year technology roadmap, uh, that is written in what we call value streams. So, if you think about an integrated immigration system, that's a complete, uh, workstream in itself. The entire tenancy bond is a complete workstream in itself. Mm-hmm. Our entire service centers are a complete workstream in themselves. Those workstreams contain a number of initiatives.

Um, sometimes they can be just simply security, making sure that they, everything's, uh, from a cyber perspective is okay. But inherently, they are focused at improving the service delivery. Two focuses, one on customer or citizen, the other is on making it easier for our people to do their jobs. We see AI, uh, as being a huge tool to actually supplement and help that.

Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Um, we've already seen a number of, uh, things introduced into our service center that has reduced administrative time on, if you like, drafting up post calls- Yeah.... where it can auto- be automatically transcribed to allow people- Yeah.... to actually get on them. So there's those kind of things- Mm-hmm.... that w- we reduce what we call the cognitive load. Um, and we've got AI embedded in, uh, lots of our, uh, platforms.

Mm-hmm. And the question is just actually turning those on, but turning those on in a safe, responsible, and ethical way. Mm-hmm. Um, I think the world's still learning about AI. And, uh, we're just making sure that we open up the tools to allow our people to be more, uh, effective, um, reduce the, what we call cognitive load, and then also just that it's safe. Great.

Yeah. Okay. Thank you. A final question for, uh, for this part, and then we will move on to part two. Irena Williams. Oh. Yeah. Only 25 minutes for this. Do you have a question for, for part one? I do. Mm-hmm. Um, I wonder if you can help us. We set aside time for this section as a committee because we, we want to understand the targets and how they've changed.

Um, and, uh, I see that that's- (coughs)... perhaps not what you anticipated. But can you help us to understand what has changed? Because last year when the committee considered this, there were some targets that were unmet that have been changed. So it looks to us like there might be some, um, you know, that perhaps one might look at it and think there was a moving of the goalpost so that, so that those could be met. And then there are some that have changed because of the government's priorities changing, and that's perfectly fine.

Um, but we don't know what those are because we didn't change them. Help us to, um, get a picture of what those targets are hitting and, and how you're performing well, and what are the things that you're still working towards, as you said, over a few years. I, I... At this point, I don't think we'll be able to go through every single target and tell you what- No, I agree.... we've got. So I do think it would probably better we come back with a written response where we highlight all the ones that have changed and the reasons why.

All right. But there's at the back on, um, 240... In fact, if... I'll go... I'll, I'll... Head to 240, the back of our book, you'll see that it runs for a number of pages where we divide them into those who have been discontinued for no funding, those who have changed, um, the location of where they're reported, those who have changed due to changes in government priorities, and those which are quite a few, it runs to...

Changes in government priorities runs to three pages. Discontinued, no funding runs to another three pages. Yeah. Um, so there's quite a few. The pages I'm, I'm assuming you want to are the ones which are around we've replaced or improved the measure. Yeah. Um, those are around ones which I think as we go through each of the areas, we could come back in terms of what...

As we talk through each of the policy areas, it might be worth then talking then about why we made those changes as we go through each of the individual policy areas. Um, most of those you'll see there's a number which have been changed because we have to align with the FRS change- Financial reporting. Yeah. ... of reporting financial reporting statement. So, they are things we just have to do from a technical point of view. Um, and some of them are, as we've talked about, changes because actually things have moved on and we are trying to reflect

more accurately what's happening, um, out in the market. Um, some are new measures because we've got a new appropriation. So there's, there's unfortunately nine pages of small print- Fair enough.... you know, we would go through. So I think it's probably best in terms of the committee's time we'll come back with a written- Yeah, so a follow-up question for the-... response for you. (laughs) Thank you. Uh, so we do... Uh, yeah, that's noted, uh, for a follow-up question. And now we move on to part two.

And I will- So I put my hand up.... turn to Honorable Jenny Anderson. Yeah. Thank you. Now am I allowed to ask my question? Yeah, you are. Um, what did the ministry do in 2024 and '25 to improve real household median weekly incomes- Next one.... um, in terms of how much money people are being able to bring in? Now you get to go.

And, and, and acknowledging too that in the context that, um, cost of living has increased- Mm-hmm.... over a period of time and that's putting strain on, on the household. So I think my main, my main answer is, is as before that uh, the, the main thing that MBIE can do is to be, uh, focusing on those medium-term policy settings that are going to, uh, um, hopefully, uh, shift the, the dial in New Zealand's productivity performance longer term. Um, there are obviously shorter term things as well, things like, um, the focus on, uh, tourism or, um,

immigration setting changes that can have more immediate effect on, um, giving, uh, impetus to the economy. But, you know, primarily there's those medium-term settings changes. I would be happy to answer other questions as well. When, um, quite often... And this isn't in any particular government, but what we see in New Zealand is, um, uh- When things are cyclical in terms of unemployment, we'll use immigration as a way of, um... And the construction sector, for example, is one where we've lost a lot of workers and, um, likely over the next

12 to 18 months, we'll, we'll be wanting to, um, attract people from other countries, fill some of the, the shortfall we have in, in upcoming projects. Do you do any analysis on what the impact is on productivity when you're having to bring in workers from other countries to fill gaps in our labor market? Does that have wider implications that you take into a, um, into account, or, or, or not really?

Um, well, from a, uh, long-term productivity perspective, the key thing from the immigration system is how targeted it is to higher skills. Yeah.
